verses.commentary: Passover week begins. Return to Bethany. Lazarus is living proof of resurrection power.
Martha serves (her character). Lazarus reclines (social proof of miracle). Honor to Jesus.
Pure nard: costly, imported. Poured, not dabbed. Feet, not head. Hair as towel: shocking intimacy. Fragrance fills house. Worship cannot be hidden.
His objection foreshadows his character. Criticism from inside the circle.
Three hundred denarii: laborer's annual income. Seemingly reasonable concern for poor.
Judas embezzled. Feigned concern masked greed. Treasurer was thief. Ministry money stolen.
Leave her alone. It was intended that she should save this perfume for the day of My burial. Jesus defends Mary. Burial preparation. She anoints Him for death. Prophetic act. The cross looms.
Poverty is chronic; His presence temporary. Opportunity for worship now. Economics bow to incarnation.
Dual attraction: Jesus and Lazarus. Living evidence. Sightseers and seekers.
Lazarus' existence threatens their control. Witnesses to resurrection must be silenced. Murderous logic.
His life produces faith in Jesus. Testimony through existence. The priests fear losing people.
News spreads. Messianic expectation builds. Entry anticipated.
Hosanna: Save now! Psalm 118:25-26. Messianic acclamation. They proclaim Him king.
Zechariah 9:9. Humble mount. King comes peacefully, not on warhorse. Intentional messianic sign.
Fear not. True king arrives. Gentle, not threatening. Salvation, not conquest.
Resurrection unlocks understanding. Glorification brings clarity. Hindsight reveals Scripture's fulfillment.
Lazarus miracle advertised. Testimony spreads. Word of mouth powerful.
Lazarus resurrection motivates crowd. Miracle creates curiosity and faith.
Admission of failure. Hyperbole: whole world. But prophetic. Gentiles will follow. John 3:16 coming true.
God-fearers. Proselytes. They come to Jerusalem. Transition begins. Judaism opens to nations.

They came to Philip, who was from Bethsaida in Galilee, with a request. Sir, we would like to see Jesus. Greek names seek Greek-named disciple. Philip from Galilee of the Gentiles. Simple request: see Jesus. Gentile mission beginning.
Disciples facilitate access. They bring Gentiles to Jesus. Apostolic role previewed.
Greeks' arrival signals timing. Universal mission requires cross. Glory through death. The hour is crucifixion.
Death produces life. One seed buried yields many. Jesus' death multiplies disciples. Fruitfulness through sacrifice.
Self-preservation destroys. Self-sacrifice saves. Hate means relative preference. This world versus eternal life.
Disciples go where Jesus goes: cross, then glory. Father honors faithful servants. Divine recognition for human faithfulness.
Psuchē (ψυχή) troubled. Tarasso (ταράσσω), deep agitation. Temptation to escape. But purpose demands the cross. For this He came.
Divine response. Audible voice. Past glory: ministry. Future glory: cross and resurrection. God speaks publicly.
Some hear thunder. Others angel. Few recognize God's voice. Revelation meets human limitation.
Jesus needs no audible confirmation. Public revelation for witnesses. They need to hear God validate Him.
Satan's defeat. Ruler of this world cast out. Cosmic battle engaged. Victory through apparent defeat.
John's double meaning (3:14, 8:28). Exaltation through execution. All people: Jews and Gentiles. Universal magnetic power. The cross attracts.
Lifted up means crucifixion. Manner of death indicated. Roman execution method.
Scripture says Messiah eternal (Psalm 89:36, Isaiah 9:7, Daniel 7:14). How can He die? Confusion over titles and timing.
Limited time. Use the light now. Darkness coming. Blindness without light. Disorientation in darkness.
Believe now. Become light bearers. He departs. Concealment. Public ministry effectively ends. The hour approaches.
Many signs. Persistent unbelief. Evidence abundant. Faith absent. Hardness of heart.
Rejection prophesied. Unbelief is ancient pattern. Messianic suffering foretold and ignored.
Not just would not but could not. Judicial blindness. God's sovereignty in unbelief.
God blinds and hardens. Not arbitrary but judicial response to persistent rejection. They cannot see, understand, or turn. Healing unavailable to hardened hearts.
Pre-incarnate glory. Old Testament prophets saw Jesus. Unity of Scripture.
Leaders included. Fear silences confession. Excommunication threat. Social cost of faith. Covert discipleship.
Love hierarchy wrong. Human approval valued over divine approval. Fear of man versus fear of God. Misplaced affection.
Belief in Jesus is belief in the Father. Unity of mission and identity.
Seeing Jesus is seeing God. Incarnation makes invisible visible. John 1:18, 14:9.
Light brings escape from darkness. Belief transfers from darkness to light. Salvation as illumination.
Jesus does not judge now. Mission is rescue. Judgment deferred.
Jesus' words are the standard. Final day brings reckoning. Rejection brings condemnation. The word judges.
Father commanded. Jesus obeyed. Words are God's words. Authority derived.
Jesus speaks exactly what Father says. Perfect transmission. Faithful messenger. Life through obedience to the word.
